title: Systems of Inequalities
blurb: Graphing two-variable inequalities, shading half-planes, and systems of inequalities as feasible regions.

lessons.title: Solutions in the Plane
lessons.concreteComparison: The inequality $y > 2x + 1$ isn't asking for one answer — it's running a border checkpoint on the entire plane. The point $(1, 5)$? $5 > 3$: admitted. $(2, 3)$? $3 > 5$ is false: turned away. Every point in the plane gets a yes or no, and the yeses form a whole REGION — half of everything.
lessons.objective: Test whether points satisfy two-variable inequalities and understand solutions as regions.
concept: A solution of a two-variable inequality is any point $(x, y)$ that makes it TRUE. Substitute both coordinates; the verdict is immediate.
$(1, 5)$ in $y > 2x + 1$: is $5 > 2(1) + 1 = 3$? Yes — solution. One substitution, one answer.
Unlike an equation's razor-thin line of solutions, an inequality's solutions fill half the plane: infinitely many points, forming one connected region.
The boundary line $y = 2x + 1$ splits the plane into two halves; the inequality claims exactly one of them (and, for $\le$ or $\ge$, the line itself).
lessons.keyIdea: Substitute the point; true means inside the solution region. Inequalities claim half the plane.

facts.title: Half the plane
facts.explanation: An inequality's solutions form an entire region.
facts.latex: boundary line splits it in two

workedExamples.solution: $12 - 4 = 8 \le 8$ ✓ — yes, exactly on the boundary, and $\le$ includes it.

commonMistakes.text: Substituting the coordinates into the wrong letters.
commonMistakes.correction: A point is (x, y) IN THAT ORDER: (1, 5) sends x = 1, y = 5. Swapping them tests a different point entirely.

commonMistakes.text: Counting boundary points as solutions of strict inequalities.
commonMistakes.correction: On y > 2x + 1, points ON the line satisfy equality, not the strict >. They belong only when the symbol is ≥ or ≤.

steps.title: Checkpoint on the plane
steps.teach: The line $y = 2x + 1$ passes through these points. Everything ABOVE it satisfies $y > 2x + 1$; everything below fails. Two of the plotted points are off the line — decide which side each lives on.

steps.title: Substitute and judge
beats: Feed BOTH coordinates in: $x$ first, $y$ second.
Every point in the plane gets a verdict.

steps.title: Half the plane says yes
beats: The boundary $y = 2x + 1$ is a fence.
One whole SIDE satisfies the inequality.
The other whole side fails.
Strict symbols exclude the fence; $\le, \ge$ include it.

steps.title: Verdicts at speed
steps.intro: x first, y second, then read the symbol.

examples.prompt: Test $(0, 0)$ in $4x + 3y < 12$ — and explain why the origin is everyone's favorite test point.
steps: The origin settles most shading questions in one breath.

examples.prompt: The line $y = -x + 4$ passes through $(1, 3)$. Which side of it is $(3, 3)$ on, relative to $y > -x + 4$?
A snack budget: $2x + 1.5y \le 12$ (x sandwiches, y juices). Can you afford 3 sandwiches and 4 juices?

problems.prompt: Which point is NOT a solution of $y < 3x$?
How many solutions does $y \le x + 2$ have?
problems.explanation: Every point on or below the line qualifies — half the plane.

steps.title: Half-planes run your thermostat
steps.body: Control engineers describe safe operating states as inequality regions: temperature under a max, pressure over a min. A device's software constantly point-tests its current state against these half-planes — thousands of substitutions per second. When your laptop fan spins up, a point just crossed a boundary line.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Point test: substitute x and y; true means member.
An inequality's solutions fill half the plane.
Strict symbols exclude the boundary; $\le, \ge$ include it.

lessons.title: Graphing Half-Planes
lessons.concreteComparison: To graph $y \le 2x - 1$, you build a fence and paint a side: draw the line $y = 2x - 1$ (SOLID, since $\le$ keeps it), test one easy point to learn which side is true, and shade that side. Fence, test, paint — three moves, and infinity is mapped.
lessons.objective: Graph two-variable inequalities: boundary line (solid/dashed), test point, and shading.
concept: Step 1 — FENCE: graph the boundary line from the related equation. Use a SOLID line for $\le, \ge$ (the fence belongs) and a DASHED line for $<, >$ (it doesn't).
Step 2 — TEST: pick an easy point off the line (the origin, if available) and substitute.
Step 3 — PAINT: shade the side containing your true test point — or the other side if it failed.
Special fences: $x > 3$ is a vertical dashed line shaded right; $y \le -2$ is horizontal solid shaded down.
Solved-form shortcut: with $y$ isolated and a POSITIVE $y$-coefficient, $y >$ shades above, $y <$ shades below. The test point never lies, though.
lessons.keyIdea: Fence (solid/dashed), test one point, paint the true side.

facts.title: Solid vs dashed
facts.explanation: Inclusive symbols keep the fence; strict ones exclude it.

facts.title: The origin trick
facts.latex: test $(0,0)$ when it's off the line

workedExamples.solution: Solid line through $(0, -1)$ with slope $2$; test $(0,0)$: $0 \le -1$ FALSE, so shade the side WITHOUT the origin (below the line).
Dashed line through $(4, 0)$ and $(0, 4)$; test $(0,0)$: $0 > 4$ false — shade the far side (above/right).
Vertical SOLID line at $x = -2$, shaded right — every point with x-coordinate at least $-2$.

commonMistakes.text: Always shading above for > without isolating y.
commonMistakes.correction: The rule 'greater shades above' needs y ISOLATED with a positive coefficient. In x − y > 2 the safe move is a test point — which shades BELOW here.

commonMistakes.text: Testing a point that's ON the boundary.
commonMistakes.correction: A boundary point gives equality — useless for choosing a side. Pick a point clearly off the fence; the origin, unless the line passes through it.

tryIt.statement: Solid or dashed, and which side: $y > \frac{1}{2}x + 3$?
tryIt.solution: Dashed (strict); test $(0,0)$: $0 > 3$ false — shade the non-origin side (above).
Horizontal dashed line at $y = 4$, shaded below.

steps.title: Two fences, four lands
steps.teach: Two boundary lines divide the plane into regions. Study where each line runs — in the next lessons you'll claim sides of each and hunt the overlap.

steps.title: Build the fence
beats: Graph the RELATED equation as the boundary.
$\le$ or $\ge$: SOLID — the fence is included.
$<$ or $>$: DASHED — points on it don't count.
The line's job: separate yes-land from no-land.

steps.title: Test, then paint
beats: Pick the easiest off-line point — usually $(0, 0)$.
True → shade the test point's side.
False → shade the OTHER side.
One honest point beats any memorized rule.

steps.title: Fence style
options: a dashed line, origin side shaded
a solid line, origin side shaded
a dashed line, far side shaded
steps.explanation: Strict $<$ → dashed. Test $(0,0)$: $0 < 6$ ✓ — shade the origin's side.

steps: Paint the far side (away from the origin).

examples.prompt: Graph $y > -2x$ (the fence runs through the origin!).
A club needs $x + y \le 20$ members across two teams, with team rosters nonnegative. Describe the full picture.
steps: Solid fence from $(20, 0)$ to $(0, 20)$, origin side shaded.
A shaded triangle — your first feasible region.

steps.title: Painter's exam
steps.intro: Style the fence, test honestly, paint once.

problems.prompt: Which inequality has a SOLID boundary with the origin side shaded?

options: above a solid line through the origin
problems.explanation: $\ge$ solid; test $(0, 5)$ (origin is on the line): $5 \ge 0$ ✓ — above.

problems.prompt: A point in the shaded region of $x - 2y > 4$ is…

steps.title: Dashed lines are a legal technology
steps.body: Cartographers used dashed vs solid boundaries centuries before algebra class did: a solid border on old maps meant settled and owned; dashed meant disputed or unsurveyed. Mathematics borrowed the convention wholesale — a dashed fence is the map's way of saying 'this edge is not yours.' Same symbol, same meaning, four hundred years apart.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Fence: solid for inclusive, dashed for strict.
Test one honest off-line point (love the origin).
Paint the true side — half the plane, claimed.

lessons.title: Systems of Inequalities
lessons.concreteComparison: One inequality paints half the plane. Two paint two overlapping halves — and the SYSTEM's solution is where the paint doubles up: the overlap. Like a treasure map's 'north of the river AND west of the ridge,' each new condition shrinks the search area. Graph each, then hunt the region wearing every color.
lessons.objective: Solve systems of linear inequalities graphically and verify points against every condition.
concept: A system's solution set is the INTERSECTION: points satisfying EVERY inequality at once.
Method: graph each half-plane (fence, test, paint), then identify the region covered by ALL shadings.
Verify candidates against every condition — a point must pass EACH inequality, and one failure ejects it.
The overlap can be a wedge, a strip, a polygon — or EMPTY, when the conditions contradict (parallel fences painting away from each other).
Corners of the region sit at fence intersections — solve the boundary equations in pairs to locate them exactly.
lessons.keyIdea: Graph every half-plane; the system's solutions live where all the shadings overlap.

facts.title: Intersection
facts.explanation: One failed test ejects a point from the system.

facts.title: Corners
facts.explanation: The region's vertices are intersections of fence lines.

workedExamples.statement: Is $(2, 3)$ a solution of the system $y > x$ and $x + y < 8$?
workedExamples.solution: $3 > 2$ ✓ and $5 < 8$ ✓ — yes, it's in the overlap.
$6 > 5$ ✓ but $11 < 8$ ✗ — one failure ejects it. No.

workedExamples.statement: Find the corner of the region bounded by $y = x$ and $x + y = 8$.
workedExamples.solution: Substitute: $x + x = 8 \to x = 4$: the corner is $(4, 4)$.

commonMistakes.text: Accepting a point that passes MOST conditions.
commonMistakes.correction: Systems are ANDs: 2 of 3 is a fail. Test every inequality, and stop at the first ✗.

commonMistakes.text: Shading the union instead of the intersection.
commonMistakes.correction: The answer isn't 'anywhere any paint landed' — it's where ALL paints stack. Trace the deepest shading.

tryIt.statement: Does $(1, 4)$ solve the system $y \ge 2x$ and $y \le x + 5$?
Find where the boundaries $y = 2x$ and $y = x + 5$ cross.

steps.title: Where the paints stack
steps.teach: Here are the fences $y = 2x$ and $y = x + 5$. Claiming ABOVE the first and BELOW the second traps a wedge between them — every point inside passes both tests. Find their crossing: that's the wedge's corner.

steps.title: Paint twice, keep the overlap
beats: Graph inequality 1: fence, test, paint.
Graph inequality 2 on the same axes.
The system lives where the shadings STACK.
Three inequalities? Three coats — same rule.

steps.title: Every gate, every time
beats: Candidate point → test against EACH inequality.
$(2,3)$: passes $y > x$, passes $x + y < 8$. In.
$(5,6)$: passes the first, fails the second. Out.
Systems have no partial credit.

steps.title: Corners are crossings
beats: Fence lines intersect at the region's corners.
Solve boundaries in PAIRS (Grade 8 systems skills).
$y = x$ meets $x + y = 8$ at $(4, 4)$.
Corners matter — next lessons live on them.

steps.title: In or out?
steps.prompt: System: $x \ge 1$, $y \ge 2$, $x + y \le 7$. Which point is a solution?

steps.title: Overlap hunting
steps.intro: Coat by coat; corners by pairs.

examples.prompt: Describe the solution region of $y \le 4$, $y \ge x$, $x \ge 0$.
steps: Below the horizontal $y = 4$; above $y = x$; right of the y-axis.

examples.prompt: Give TWO solutions and TWO non-solutions of $y > x - 2$ and $y < x + 3$.
steps: The strip between two parallel fences.
Out: $(0, 4)$ (busts the upper) and $(4, 0)$ (busts the lower).

examples.prompt: Why does the system $y > x + 1$ and $y < x - 1$ have NO solutions?
steps: First demands ABOVE the upper line; second demands BELOW the lower.
The claims point away from each other: empty overlap.

problems.prompt: How many of these points solve BOTH $y \ge -x + 3$ and $y \le 5$: $(1, 2), (3, 4), (6, 6)$?

problems.explanation: Four fences box in the first-quadrant rectangle with corners up to $(4, 3)$.

problems.prompt: Where do the boundaries $y = 3x - 2$ and $y = x + 6$ cross?

steps.title: Air traffic flies through overlaps
steps.body: A plane's allowed position is a system of inequalities: above minimum altitude, below its ceiling, inside its corridor's walls, outside every other plane's protected bubble. Air traffic control software re-tests thousands of these point-in-region questions every second. The 'overlap of all constraints' you just shaded is, at 10,000 meters, the definition of safe.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A system's solutions are the INTERSECTION of its half-planes.
Test candidates against every condition — one failure ejects.
Corners sit at boundary crossings; solve fence pairs to find them.

lessons.title: Writing Constraint Systems
lessons.concreteComparison: A bake sale: brownies take $2$ eggs each, cookies take $1$, and you have $24$ eggs. Oven time: $20$ min per brownie batch, $15$ per cookie batch, at most $300$ minutes. You can't bake negative anything. That's FOUR inequalities before you've measured a cup of flour — real plans are constraint systems, and writing them is the modeling skill.
lessons.objective: Translate multi-constraint stories into systems of inequalities, including natural nonnegativity constraints.
concept: Name the variables first, with units: $x$ = brownie batches, $y$ = cookie batches.
Each RESOURCE contributes one inequality: (per-unit use)·(count) summed $\le$ supply. Eggs: $2x + y \le 24$; oven: $20x + 15y \le 300$.
Add the invisible constraints reality insists on: $x \ge 0$, $y \ge 0$ — counts can't go negative. Some stories also demand minimums ('at least 4 cookie batches': $y \ge 4$).
Requirements use $\ge$ (must have at least), capacities use $\le$ (can't exceed). The story's verbs pick the direction.
The finished system IS the plan's rulebook; any point satisfying all of it is a legal plan.
lessons.keyIdea: One inequality per resource or requirement, plus nonnegativity — the story's rulebook in algebra.

facts.title: Resource rows
facts.explanation: Each limited resource writes one inequality.

facts.title: The invisible pair
facts.explanation: Reality's free constraints — never forget them.

workedExamples.statement: Write the bake-sale system: eggs ($2$/brownie batch, $1$/cookie batch, $24$ total), oven ($20$ and $15$ min, $300$ total).
Check: can the sale make $6$ brownie batches and $8$ cookie batches?
A trainer schedules cardio ($c$) and weights ($w$) hours: at least $3$ h cardio, at least $2$ h weights, at most $10$ h total. Write the system.

commonMistakes.correction: Without x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0 the algebra happily 'bakes' −3 batches to free up eggs. Reality's constraints must be written down.
'At most 300 oven-minutes' is ≤; 'at least 4 batches' is ≥. Swap them and the rulebook demands waste while banning work.

tryIt.statement: A shelf holds at most $18$ kg; hardcovers weigh $1.2$ kg, paperbacks $0.4$ kg. Write the weight constraint.
Check: do $10$ hardcovers and $15$ paperbacks fit?

steps.title: Variables with units
beats: $x$ = brownie batches, $y$ = cookie batches.
Units first — every later row inherits them.
Two variables → a plane of possible plans.
The constraints will carve out the legal ones.

steps.title: One resource, one row
beats: Eggs: $2x + 1y \le 24$.
Pattern: per-unit use × count, summed, $\le$ supply.
Requirements flip the symbol: at least → $\ge$.

steps.title: The free constraints
beats: $x \ge 0$, $y \ge 0$: no negative baking.
They pin the region into the first quadrant.
Whole-number needs? Note them — the graph stays continuous, the ANSWER gets rounded.
Rulebook complete: four rows, one legal region.

steps.title: Write the row
steps.prompt: A van carries at most $900$ kg: crates ($x$) weigh $30$ kg, barrels ($y$) weigh $75$ kg. The weight row is…
steps.explanation: Per-unit weights times counts, capped by capacity: $\le$.

steps.title: Rulebooks, drafted
steps.intro: Variables, resource rows, requirement rows, reality rows.

examples.prompt: A florist makes bouquets: small uses $5$ roses, large uses $9$; supply $180$ roses; a standing order requires at least $6$ large. System?
Verify the florist can fill $18$ small and $10$ large.
A student budgets study time: math at least twice the science time, total at most $12$ h, science at least $2$ h. System (m, s)?

steps.title: Draft the rulebook
steps.intro: Resources cap, requirements floor, reality zeroes.

problems.prompt: A budget allows at most \$60: apps cost \$4, games \$12. Which system row is WRONG?
problems.explanation: The counts must be WEIGHTED by prices — $a + g \le 60$ caps the item count at 60, not the spend.

problems.prompt: 'At least $5$ volunteers per shift' translates to…
A plan $(x, y) = (4, -1)$ satisfies every resource row. Verdict?
problems.explanation: Negative production is reality-banned; the invisible constraints eject it.

steps.title: The diet problem
steps.body: In 1945, economist George Stigler posed the 'diet problem': meet every nutrient requirement (a stack of ≥ rows) as cheaply as possible. He solved 77 foods and 9 nutrients by hand-guessing; today the same constraint-writing feeds prisons, armies, and hospital kitchens by computer. The answer, then and now, is depressingly heavy on flour and cabbage — constraints are honest even when dinner isn't exciting.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Each resource caps a weighted sum ($\le$); each requirement floors one ($\ge$).
Never omit $x \ge 0, y \ge 0$ — reality's free rows.
The finished system is the plan's rulebook; test plans row by row.

lessons.title: Feasible Regions
lessons.concreteComparison: Graph the bake sale's four inequalities and the legal plans form a neat quadrilateral — the **feasible region**. Every interior point is a workable plan; everything outside breaks a rule. The region's CORNERS are special: they're the plans that push two resources to their limits at once, and they're where the best plans hide.
lessons.objective: Graph feasible regions from constraint systems and find their corner points.
concept: The **feasible region** is the overlap of all constraint half-planes — the geometry of every legal plan at once.
With nonnegativity, regions live in the first quadrant, typically polygons: triangles, quadrilaterals, sometimes unbounded wedges.
**Corner points** (vertices) occur where two boundary lines cross; find them by solving those two equations as a system.
Corners are maximal-effort plans: each one uses up two constraints completely. Interior points leave slack everywhere.
Sketch → label each fence → shade the overlap → solve pairs for corners. That inventory of corners is the region's skeleton.
lessons.keyIdea: The feasible region is the constraints' overlap; its corners come from solving boundary pairs.

facts.title: Corners exhaust pairs
facts.explanation: Solve the two boundary equations to pin each vertex.

workedExamples.solution: The axes and one slanted fence: corners $(0,0)$, $(6,0)$, $(0,6)$ — a triangle.
Axis corners $(0,0), (4,0), (0,5)$; the slanted fences cross where $2x + y = 8$ and $x + 2y = 10$: $x = 2, y = 4$. Four corners: $(0,0), (4,0), (2,4), (0,5)$.

workedExamples.statement: Is the region of $x \ge 1$ and $y \ge x$ bounded or unbounded?
workedExamples.solution: Unbounded — the wedge opens forever up and right; no ceiling fence closes it.

commonMistakes.text: Reporting fence intersections that aren't corners.
commonMistakes.correction: Two boundaries may cross OUTSIDE the region (another constraint cuts first). Verify each candidate corner against ALL constraints before listing it.

commonMistakes.text: Solving corner systems by guessing from the sketch.
commonMistakes.correction: Sketches lie at close quarters. Corners come from ALGEBRA — solve the two boundary equations exactly.

tryIt.statement: Where do $3x + y = 9$ and $x + y = 5$ cross?

steps.title: Fences into a pen
steps.teach: These two fences — plus the axes — pen in a quadrilateral. Track where each pair crosses; those crossings are the feasible region's corners.

steps.title: Legal plans have geometry
beats: Each constraint slices the plane.
The survivors' overlap: the feasible region.
First-quadrant polygons, usually.
Interior = slack everywhere; edges = one resource maxed; corners = two.

steps.title: Vertices by algebra
beats: Pick two boundary EQUATIONS.
Solve the little system (substitution or elimination).
Reject crossings that violate any OTHER constraint.

steps.title: Count the corners
steps.prompt: The region $x \ge 0, y \ge 0, x + y \le 5, y \le 3$ has how many corners?
steps.explanation: $(0,0), (5,0), (2,3), (0,3)$ — the fence $y = 3$ clips the triangle into a quadrilateral.

examples.prompt: Map the bake-sale region: $2x + y \le 24$, $20x + 15y \le 300$, $x, y \ge 0$.
Why is $(15, 0)$ — the oven fence's x-intercept — NOT a corner of that region?
steps: The egg fence cuts first along the x-axis: $(12, 0)$ is the true corner.

examples.prompt: A region has corners $(0,0), (8,0), (5,6), (0,9)$. Which corner maxes BOTH slanted resources?
steps: Axis corners each leave a resource idle.
$(5,6)$ sits at the crossing of the two slanted fences.

steps.title: Vertex inventory
steps.intro: Solve pairs; audit each candidate against the full rulebook.

problems.prompt: The region $x \ge 2, y \ge 1, x + y \le 4$ is…
problems.explanation: Three fences, three corners — check each satisfies all rows.

problems.prompt: A candidate corner fails one constraint by a hair. It is…
options: not a corner of the region
problems.explanation: Corners must satisfy EVERY constraint — the region's skeleton has no exceptions.

steps.title: The simplex crawl
steps.body: George Dantzig's 1947 simplex algorithm — still the workhorse of industrial planning — solves gigantic constraint systems by CRAWLING along the feasible region's edges from corner to corner, always improving. Airlines, refineries, and shipping fleets run it daily on regions with millions of fences. The corner-hunting you did by hand is the same walk, at planetary scale.

steps.title: What you learned
points: The feasible region is the overlap — legal plans as geometry.
Corners come from solving boundary pairs exactly.
Audit every candidate corner against the FULL rulebook.

lessons.title: The Best Plan in the Region
lessons.concreteComparison: The bake sale's region holds hundreds of legal plans — but profit is \$9 per brownie batch and \$5 per cookie batch, and you want the MOST money. Here's the miracle: you don't check hundreds of plans. The best one always sits at a CORNER of the region. Evaluate profit at four corners, crown the winner. That's optimization — Grade 9 edition.
lessons.objective: Evaluate an objective at a feasible region's corners to find maximum or minimum plans.
concept: An **objective** scores every plan: profit $P = 9x + 5y$, or cost, or time. Optimization hunts the best score among LEGAL plans.
**Corner principle:** for a linear objective over a polygon region, the max and min always occur at corners. (Sliding a constant-profit line across the region, the last touch is a vertex.)
Method: list the corners (last lesson's skill), evaluate the objective at each, compare.
Ties happen: two corners scoring equally means every plan on the edge between them is optimal too.
Context still rules: if plans must be whole numbers and the best corner isn't, check the nearby whole-number plans INSIDE the region.
lessons.keyIdea: Score the corners, crown the best — linear objectives peak at vertices.

facts.title: Corner principle
facts.explanation: A sliding score-line leaves the region at a corner.

facts.title: The method
facts.explanation: Optimization reduces to a short table.

workedExamples.statement: Bake sale: corners $(0,0), (12,0), (6,12), (0,20)$; profit $P = 9x + 5y$. Find the best plan.
workedExamples.solution: $P$: $0$, $108$, $114$, $100$. Best: $(6, 12)$ — 6 brownie and 12 cookie batches for \$114.

workedExamples.statement: Same region, new prices: $P = 10x + 5y$. Now what?
workedExamples.solution: $P$: $0$, $120$, $120$, $100$. TIE between $(12,0)$ and $(6,12)$ — the whole edge between them is optimal.

commonMistakes.text: Evaluating the objective at interior points 'to be safe'.
commonMistakes.correction: The corner principle is a theorem, not a hunch — interior points never beat the best corner for a linear objective.

commonMistakes.text: Optimizing over crossings outside the region.
commonMistakes.correction: Only TRUE corners (passing every constraint) enter the table. A high score at an illegal point is a fantasy plan.

tryIt.solution: $0, 40, 32, 12$: the winner moves to $(5, 0)$ — objectives choose their own champions.

steps.title: Why corners win
beats: Fix a profit level: $9x + 5y = 60$ is a LINE.
Raise the profit: the line slides parallel.
The last point it touches, leaving the region…
…is a corner. Always. That's the whole theorem.

steps.title: Four evaluations, one crown
beats: Champion: $(6, 12)$ at \$114.
Hundreds of plans, four computations.

steps.title: Ties and integers
beats: Equal top scores → the whole edge between them is optimal.
Fractional best corner but whole-number plans required?
Check nearby integer points INSIDE the region.
Objectives change champions — recompute when prices move.

steps.title: Crown it
steps.explanation: $P$: $0, 20, 21$ — and $(4,3)$ isn't even in the region (it's no corner).

steps.title: Optimization drills
steps.intro: Corners in, table out, champion crowned.

examples.prompt: A workshop: chairs ($x$) and tables ($y$), region corners $(0,0), (10,0), (6,4), (0,7)$. Profit \$30/chair, \$50/table. Best output?
A shipper minimizes fuel $F = 2x + 5y$ over corners $(1,2), (4,1), (6,3)$.
steps: For minima, small corners near the origin usually star.

examples.prompt: Prices swing: with $P = 4x + 4y$ over corners $(0,0), (8,0), (5,3), (0,8)$, who wins?
steps: Every plan on the boundary from $(8,0)$ through $(5,3)$ to $(0,8)$ is optimal.

steps.title: Crown the corner
steps.intro: Table the corners; the objective picks its champion.

problems.prompt: The best corner is $(4.5, 3.5)$ but plans must be whole. You should…
options: test nearby integer points inside the region
problems.explanation: Rounding can exit the region; audit the integer neighbors and keep the best legal one.

steps.title: Optimization won a Nobel
steps.body: Leonid Kantorovich began optimizing plywood-factory output in 1939 with exactly this machinery — regions, corners, objectives — and shared the 1975 Nobel in economics for it. During WWII the same math routed Allied convoys; today it schedules kidneys to transplant patients. The four-row corner table you just filled in is the seed of all of it.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Linear objectives peak (and bottom out) at region corners.
Method: corner list → objective table → champion.
Ties crown whole edges; integer demands mean auditing neighbors.

practice.solution: Dashed line through $(0,3)$ with slope $-\frac{1}{2}$; $(0,0)$ gives $0 < 3$ ✓ — shade the origin side (below).

practice.statement: Write the system: a backpack holds at most $7$ kg; books weigh $0.9$ kg, binders $0.5$ kg; at least $2$ binders required.
A region's boundaries cross at $(6, 5)$, but the point violates a third constraint. Is it a corner? What should you do?
practice.solution: Not a corner — audit failed. Exclude it and find where the third fence clips the region instead.

testYourself.solution: $(2,0)$: $10 < 10$ ✗ (boundary, strict). $(4,5)$: $10 < 10$ ✗ — also boundary! $(0,-5)$: $10 < 10$ ✗. All three sit exactly ON the dashed fence — none are solutions.

testYourself.statement: Graph description: $x \ge -1$ and $y < 2$ — what does the overlap look like?
testYourself.solution: Right of the solid vertical $x = -1$, below the dashed horizontal $y = 2$: an open-cornered quarter-plane.

testYourself.statement: Check whether $(3, 2)$ is feasible for: $2x + y \le 10$, $x + 3y \le 12$, $x, y \ge 0$.

testYourself.solution: $(0,0), (4,0), (2,3), (0,4)$ — the slanted fences cross at $3x + 2y = 12$, $x + 2y = 8$: subtract to get $2x = 4$, so $(2, 3)$.

testYourself.statement: A snack stand: hot dogs ($x$, \$2 profit) and burgers ($y$, \$3 profit); grill fits at most $60$ items; supplies limit burgers to $25$; at least $10$ hot dogs promised. Write the system and find the best plan.
